era_context: The early-to-mid 12th century is marked by the Crusades, feudal consolidation in Europe, the Song-Jin division in East Asia, and the rise of scholasticism. In 1136, the English Anarchy is in its second year; the Zengids are consolidating power in the Levant; and the Song Dynasty is establishing itself as a southern Chinese state.

events.title: Stephen of England defeats Matilda's forces at Battle of Lincoln (DISPUTED DATE)
events.description: NOTE: The Battle of Lincoln is traditionally dated to February 1141, not 1136. In 1136, Stephen was consolidating his early rule. This entry is included to flag a potential dating confusion. No major battle between Stephen and Matilda's forces is reliably dated to 1136.
events.certainty_note: This event does not belong in 1136. It is included to document a historiographic error.

events.title: Anarchy in England: Stephen consolidates early rule
events.description: In 1136, King Stephen of England was in the second year of his reign, which had begun in 1135 following the death of Henry I. Stephen faced challenges to his authority from various barons and from Matilda's faction, but in 1136 he was still consolidating his position. The civil war that would define the Anarchy had not yet reached its peak; that would come later in the 1130s and 1140s.
key_figures: Matilda (claimant to English throne, not yet actively campaigning)
events.certainty_note: Stephen's early rule in 1136 is documented in contemporary English chronicles. The year 1136 is the second year of his reign.

events.title: Zengid consolidation: Zengi controls Mosul and Aleppo
events.description: Imad al-Din Zengi (r. 1127–1146) continued to consolidate Zengid power in 1136. By this year, Zengi controlled major cities including Mosul and Aleppo, making the Zengids the dominant Islamic power in the Levant and Mesopotamia. This consolidation was crucial for Islamic resistance to Crusader expansion.
events.certainty_note: Zengid consolidation is well-documented in Islamic sources. The year 1136 falls within Zengi's reign and represents a period of stable rule.

events.title: Song Dynasty establishes southern capital at Hangzhou
events.description: By 1136, the Southern Song Dynasty had firmly established Hangzhou (Lin'an) as its capital, replacing the lost northern capitals. Hangzhou became one of the most prosperous cities in the medieval world, a center of commerce, culture, and administration. The establishment of this capital represented the Song Dynasty's adaptation to its reduced territory and the beginning of a period of cultural and economic flourishing in southern China.

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)
Song Shi (History of the Song Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The establishment of Hangzhou as the Song capital is well-documented in Chinese historical records. By 1136, this was an established fact.
cross_references: 1127-001 (Jin conquest of Northern Song, leading to southern relocation)

events.title: Geoffrey of Anjou campaigns in Normandy
events.description: Geoffrey of Anjou (1113–1151), father of the future Henry II of England, was active in Normandy during 1136. Geoffrey was consolidating Angevin power in Normandy and Anjou, laying the groundwork for the vast Angevin Empire that would be established under his son. His military campaigns and political maneuvering in 1136 were part of this broader consolidation.
key_figures: King Stephen of England (rival for Norman influence)

sources.name: Chronica Monasterii Casinensis (Chronicle of Monte Cassino)

events.certainty_note: Geoffrey of Anjou's activities in Normandy during 1136 are attested in contemporary sources, though specific events are not always precisely dated.
cross_references: 1152-001 (Geoffrey's son Henry marries Eleanor of Aquitaine)

disconfirming_evidence: The entry for 1136-001 (Battle of Lincoln) is included specifically to flag a historiographic error. The Battle of Lincoln is universally dated to February 1141, not 1136. No major battle between Stephen and Matilda's forces is reliably dated to 1136. This error may arise from confusion in some medieval sources or from miscopying of dates.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1136 is dominated by Western European sources, particularly English chronicles documenting Stephen's early reign. Islamic sources provide information on Zengid consolidation but are less detailed for specific events in 1136. Chinese sources are comprehensive for Song Dynasty developments. The record is biased toward political and military events; economic and cultural developments are underrepresented. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and Central Asia are essentially absent from contemporary documentation.

graph_edges.note: Stephen's consolidation in 1136 is part of his early reign that began in 1135
Zengid consolidation in 1136 is a consequence of Zengi's rise to power
The Jin conquest of 1127 led to the Song Dynasty's relocation to Hangzhou by 1136
Geoffrey of Anjoy's consolidation of Angevin power in 1136 laid the groundwork for his son Henry's marriage to Eleanor in 1152
